explanations: Verbs which have the base ending in -e are conjugated somewhat differently from what has been learnt so far. They can have two verbal bases, one ending in -e, the other ending in -aya, as in coreti and corayati.

Gerunds and infinitives from verbs with bases ending in -e are formed retaining the -e in the base. For example, gerunds: desetvā, cintetvā, pūjetvā, pūretvā, etc.; infinitives: desetuṃ, cintetuṃ, pūjetuṃ, pūretuṃ, etc.

Verbs which have the base ending in -nā are conjugated as shown with the base kiṇā (to buy). Some verbs similarly conjugated are: vikkiṇāti, suṇāti, mināti, gaṇhāti, uggaṇhāti, jānāti, jināti, pāpuṇāti/pappoti, ocināti, pahiṇāti.
It should be observed that the present tense verbal terminations remain constant. Only the vikaraṇa suffix, or the conjugational sign in between the root and the termination, shows variation.
Special attention should be paid to the gerunds and infinitives of jānāti, suṇāti, pāpuṇāti/pappoti, and gaṇhāti, which have irregular forms.

The two verbs bhavati/hoti (to be) and karoti (to do) occur frequently in the language. Their gerunds are bhavitvā/hutvā and katvā respectively; their infinitives are bhavituṃ/hotuṃ and kātuṃ respectively.
The verb atthi (to be, from root as) and karoti (to do, from root kṛ) are special verbs of frequent occurrence and are conjugated as shown in the tables.

tables.title: Present Tense Active Voice: base core (to steal) - coreti
Present Tense Active Voice: base coraya (to steal) - corayati
Present Tense Active Voice: base kiṇā (to buy)

pali_to_english: Buddho vihārasmiṃ sannipatantānaṃ manussānaṃ dhammaṃ deseti.
Buddhassa pūjetuṃ cintento upāsako pupphāni ocināti.
Te patte udakena pūrentā gītaṃ gāyanti.
Tumhe araññe vasante mige pīḷetvā asappurisā hotha.
Mayaṃ āpaṇaṃ gantvā vāṇijehi saddhiṃ kathetvā dhaññaṃ vikkiṇāma.
Tvaṃ uḍḍentaṃ sukaṃ disvā gaṇhituṃ icchasi.
Pabbatamhā udentaṃ candaṃ passituṃ kumāro gharamhā dhāvati.
Ahaṃ kassakehi saha khettasmiṃ rukkhe ropemi.
Mayaṃ amaccehi saha mantentā pāsādasmiṃ āsanesu nisīdāma.
Tumhe Tathāgatassa sāvake nimantetvā dānaṃ detha.
Upāsakā vihāraṃ gantvā dīpe jāletvā dhammaṃ sotuṃ nisīdanti.
Luddako sīsaṃ (head) dussena chādetvā nisīditvā sakuṇe maretuṃ ussahati.
So vane āhiṇḍante goṇe gāmaṃ ānetvā vāṇijānaṃ vikkiṇāti.
Tvaṃ āpaṇehi bhaṇḍāni kiṇitvā sakaṭena ānetvā gehe ṭhapesi.
Tumhe kakacehi rukkhe chinditvā pabbatamhā pātetha.
Dhammena manusse pālentā bhūpālā akusalaṃ parivajjenti.
Saccaṃ ñātuṃ icchanto ahaṃ samaṇehi pañhe pucchāmi.
Dānaṃ datvā sīlaṃ rakkhantā sappurisā saggalokaṃ pāpuṇanti.
Dhaññaṃ minanto kassako āpaṇaṃ netvā dhaññaṃ vikkiṇituṃ cinteti.
Ahaṃ pattena pānīyaṃ pivanto dvārasmiṃ ṭhatvā maggaṃ olokemi.
So āpaṇamhā khīraṃ kiṇituṃ puttaṃ pahiṇāti.
Mayaṃ dhammaṃ uggaṇhituṃ ussahantā paṇḍitena saha mantema.
Corehi saddhiṃ gehe bhinditvā manusse pīḷentā tumhe asappurisā hotha.
Ahaṃ suvaṇṇaṃ pariyesamāne dīpamhā āgacchante vāṇije jānāmi.
Ahaṃ ācariyo homi, tvaṃ vejjo hosi.
Tvaṃ asappurisa, Buddhena desentaṃ dhammaṃ sutvā sappuriso bhavituṃ ussahasi.
Ahaṃ paṇḍitehi saddhiṃ mantento dhammena dīpaṃ pālento bhūpālo asmi.
Varāhe mārentā corā kassake pīḷentā pāpakammāni karonti.
Sīlaṃ rakkhantā puññakammāni karontā manussā saggaṃ pappotuṃ ākaṅkhanti.
Akusalaṃ pahāya pāpaṃ parivajjetvā viharantā narā sappurisā bhavanti.
english_to_pali: Having picked fruits from the trees you send (them) to the market.
Having heard the Buddha preach the doctrine I become glad.
Thinking of collecting corn I go to the field with the farmer.
Singing songs you (pl.) look at the birds flying in the sky.
I advise the wicked man who oppresses the farmers in the village.
We dig pits to plant trees in the park.
We know the man who is lighting lamps in the monastery.
You (pl.) cross the sea with sailors to reach the island.
The king governing the island wins.
We begin to learn the dhamma from recluses living in the village.
Searching for the truth the wise man goes from city to city.
Avoiding the sleeping dog with his foot the child runs home.
Wishing to be born in heaven wise men fear to do evil.
Departing from the human world wicked men are born in hell (narake).
Having invited the hermit from the mountain the king gives him a robe.
Trying to understand the truth lay devotees become recluses.
Expecting to hear the monk preaching the dhamma lay devotees assemble in the monastery.
We see with our eyes, hear with our ears (sotehi), touch with our bodies.
I am the king governing the islands.
You (pl.) are wicked men who take counsel with thieves.
Good men begin to plant trees to protect the world.
Having heard the dhamma, the thief wishes to avoid evil.
Merchants keep clothes in shops to sell (them) to farmers coming from the villages.
The sick man (gilāna) is a messenger of the gods in the human world.
There are good men in the world who admonish wicked men.
Having picked lotuses from the water, the doctor goes to the monastery to listen to the dhamma.
Seeing the Buddha and being pleased the thief throws away the arrows.
Wishing to avoid evil I practise virtue.
We cook rice to give alms to the monks coming from the monastery.
You (pl.) go from island to island searching for gold with merchants.
